description: In arithmetic, 'kelai' closes a result clause or states equality between two explicit quantities. After an operation, the answer stands in its own clause; in plain equality, 'nela' keeps the two sides audible. 'kolo' (equal) compares things on a stated basis outside this arithmetic construction.
articulatory_notes: For 'ke', press the tongue back to the soft palate and release unvoiced 'k' into middle-height front 'e'. Move the tip to the alveolar ridge for 'la', with lateral airflow opening into stressed central 'a'. Hold the hiatus before raising the tongue front for final high-front 'i' in its own syllable.
sound_symbolism: The opening 'kel' recalls 'kelu' (become), while the separate final 'i' gives the answer one more clear release. The kinship helps with result clauses even though plain equality describes a relation rather than a change.
usage_notes: A result follows its operation as '[first operand] nela [second operand] [operation]. [result] kelai.' Plain equality instead uses '[left quantity] nela [right quantity] kelai.' Both operands remain explicit when context has not already supplied one of them.

examples.phi: ta shao ta nela ta shao ta kelai.

semantic_domains.cognition: the stated answer to an arithmetic operation
semantic_domains.quantity: equality between quantities or the result of a calculation
